data.history: The Al Abu Risha family represents multiple distinct lineages sharing the Abu Risha name across different regions of the Arab world, rather than a unified genealogical line. In Iraq, the family is part of the Dulaim tribe within the larger Anizah confederation. The Iraqi branch gained particular prominence during the Iraq War when brothers Abdul Sattar Buzaigh al-Rishawi and Ahmed Bezaa Abu Risha emerged as key Sunni tribal leaders in Al-Anbar province. Abdul Sattar founded the Anbar Salvation Council and led the Sahwa (Awakening) movement that fought against al-Qaeda militants. His leadership was cut short when he was assassinated by a roadside bomb near his home in Ramadi on September 13, 2007. Following his brother's death, Ahmed Abu Risha was selected by the sheiks of Anbar province to take over leadership of the Anbar Salvation Council. Born in 1969, Ahmed continued the anti-al-Qaeda struggle while transitioning into Iraqi politics. His Iraq Awakening and Independents National Alliance won 8 of 29 seats in the 2009 Anbar governorate election, though later electoral performances were mixed. Ahmed has been a vocal critic of Iranian interference in Iraq and has supported secular, nationalistic parties over religious ones. He has maintained significant influence in Anbar politics while advocating for Sunni rights and opposing sectarian divisions. In Syria, a separate Abu Risha lineage produced the renowned poet and diplomat Omar Abu Risha (1910-1990), who was born into a wealthy literary family in Manbij, near Aleppo. Omar served as Syrian ambassador to multiple countries including Brazil, Argentina, Chile, India, Austria, and the United States from 1961 to 1964. He wrote the lyrics to 'Fī Sabīli al-Majd' (In Pursuit of Glory), which became Syria's de facto national anthem, and was considered one of the most prominent Arab poets of his era.
data.description: The Al Abu Risha represents distinct family branches across Iraq and Syria sharing the Abu Risha name. The Iraqi branch, part of the Dulaim tribe, gained prominence through leadership of the Anbar Awakening movement against al-Qaeda, while the Syrian branch produced notable literary and diplomatic figures.
data.originStory: The family name derives from 'Abu Risha' meaning 'father of the feather,' referring to an ancestral leader who distinguished themselves in tribal affairs. However, this appears to represent multiple distinct lineages rather than a unified origin story, with separate branches emerging in Iraq within the Dulaim tribal system and in Syria from a literary family background.
data.modernStatus: Ahmed Abu Risha continues as a prominent political figure in Iraqi Anbar province, leading coalitions and maintaining influence in local governance while opposing Iranian influence. The Syrian branch's modern status is unclear from available research.
data.nameEtymology: Abu Risha means 'father of the feather' in Arabic, referring to an ancestral leader who distinguished himself in tribal affairs

data.history: The Al Khalifa (House of Khalifa) is the ruling royal family of the Kingdom of Bahrain, belonging to the Anizah tribe and professing Sunni Islam. They were part of the Utub alliance that migrated from Najd in central Arabia to Kuwait in 1716, after being expelled from Umm Qasr by the Ottomans due to predatory activities against caravans in Basra and trading ships in the Shatt al-Arab waterway. Around the 1760s, the Al Khalifa and Al Jalahma migrated from Kuwait to Zubarah in modern-day Qatar, where they built and ruled the settlement. The Al Khalifa conquered Bahrain from Persian control in 1783 under Ahmed bin Muhammad bin Khalifa, following their victory at the 1782 Battle of Zubarah. Ahmed ruled as the first hakim of Bahrain until his death in 1796, spending summers in Bahrain while maintaining his base in Zubarah. After Ahmed's death, his sons Salman and Abdulla moved permanently to Bahrain and co-ruled it, with Salman settling on Bahrain Island and Abdulla on Muharraq Island. The family split into two competing branches - Al-Salman and Al-Abdulla - who engaged in open conflict between 1842 and 1846, with the Al-Salman branch emerging victorious. The Al-Abdulla descendants remained in Qatar, while the Al-Salman descendants continued ruling Bahrain. The Al Khalifa were formally recognized by the United Kingdom as rulers of Bahrain in 1820 through a treaty relationship, eventually becoming a British protectorate. The family has ruled Bahrain continuously since 1783, with the current monarch being King Hamad bin Isa Al Khalifa, who became Emir in 1999 and proclaimed himself King in 2002, establishing Bahrain as a constitutional monarchy.
data.description: The House of Khalifa is the ruling royal family of the Kingdom of Bahrain, having governed the archipelago continuously since their conquest in 1783. Descended from the Anizah tribe's Utub branch, they represent one of the Gulf's oldest continuous ruling dynasties under King Hamad bin Isa Al Khalifa.
data.originStory: The Al Khalifa trace their origins to the Anizah tribe of Najd in central Arabia. They were part of the Utub tribal alliance that was expelled from Umm Qasr by the Ottomans in the early 18th century due to their predatory activities against merchant caravans and trading vessels. This forced migration led them first to Kuwait in 1716, then to Qatar around the 1760s where they established Zubarah, before finally conquering and settling in Bahrain in 1783.
data.modernStatus: The Al Khalifa currently rule Bahrain as a constitutional monarchy under King Hamad bin Isa Al Khalifa. As of 2025, roughly half of Bahrain's cabinet ministers are Al Khalifa family members, including Prime Minister Salman bin Hamad Al Khalifa (the Crown Prince and King's son). The family maintains formal governance through the Ruling Family Council, established in 1932 and restructured in 1973 as a formal state organ.

data.history: The research provided focuses entirely on different entities than the Al Rashid (House of Rashid) family that ruled the Emirate of Jabal Shammar. The research covers Harun al-Rashid (8th-9th century Abbasid Caliph), members of the Al Maktoum family of Dubai, Al Nuaimi rulers of Ajman, and one reference to Muhammad bin Abdullah Al Rashid (1869-1897) who was an Emir of Jabal Shammar. This Muhammad Al Rashid defeated the Saudi State in the battle of Mulayda in 1891, ruling from Hail and controlling nearly two-thirds of Arabia including Qassim, Unaizah, Buraidah and Riyadh. He was the son of Abdullah bin Rashid, founder of the Emirate, and ruled the most influential period of the Emirate of Jabal Shammar from 1869 to 1897. The research confirms he was known as 'Muhammad the Great' and died of natural causes in Hail on 28 November 1897, succeeded by his adopted son and nephew Abdulaziz. However, the bulk of research concerns entirely different families and historical figures, providing no comprehensive information about the broader Al Rashid dynasty's origins, tribal connections, migration patterns, or other notable members beyond this single ruler.
data.description: The Al Rashid, or House of Rashid, was a powerful ruling dynasty that controlled the Emirate of Jabal Shammar in northern Arabia from 1836 to 1921. The research confirms they were based in Hail and at their peak controlled vast territories across Arabia.
data.originStory: The existing data indicates the Al Rashid dynasty was established by Abdullah bin Rashid around 1836 when he consolidated power over the Shammar tribal confederation in the Ha'il oasis, though the provided research does not contain details to verify this origin story.

data.history: The Al-Fadhil family represents the descendants of the Al Fadl tribe, a powerful Arab confederation that dominated the Syrian Desert and steppe during the Middle Ages. The family traces its origins to Fadl ibn Rabi'ah, who descended from the Banu Tayy through his ancestor Mufarrij al-Jarrah. The Banu Rabi'ah were themselves descendants of the 10th-century Jarrahid rulers of Palestine, establishing a clear lineage from the ancient Tayy confederation that originally migrated from Yemen to northern Arabia in the late 2nd century CE, settling in the Shammar and Salma Mountains. The Al Fadl rose to prominence during the Crusader period by assisting the Burids and Zengids against the Crusaders, leveraging their strategic position to gain political influence. As hereditary holders of the office of Amir al-ʿarab under various rulers, they controlled extensive territories stretching from Homs in the west to Qal'at Ja'bar in the east, and from the Euphrates valley in the north to central Arabia in the south. Their dominance in the Syrian Desert made them crucial intermediaries for trade routes and political alliances between Egypt, Syria, Arabia, and Iraq. The family's power base was built on their ability to control Bedouin tribes across northern Syria and their territories between major urban centers, making them indispensable to successive Islamic dynasties who needed to maintain control over the desert routes and nomadic populations.
data.description: Al-Fadhil represents the modern descendants of the medieval Al Fadl Arab tribe that dominated the Syrian Desert during the Middle Ages as hereditary Amir al-ʿarab under various Islamic dynasties. They controlled extensive territories in Syria and northern Arabia and served as rulers of Bedouin tribes across the region.
data.originStory: Descended from Fadl ibn Rabi'ah, who was a descendant of the Banu Tayy through his ancestor Mufarrij al-Jarrah. The Banu Rabi'ah were descendants of the 10th-century Jarrahid rulers of Palestine. The lineage traces back to the ancient Tayy confederation that migrated from Yemen to northern Arabia in the late 2nd century CE, settling in the Shammar and Salma Mountains.

data.history:
The Al-Thawadi (also spelled Al-Thawawda or Al-Thawawida in Arabic: الذواودة/الذوادي) is an Arab tribe with origins tracing back to the Bani Khalid tribal confederation, not Bani Tamim as previously recorded. The Bani Khalid were a major Arab tribal confederation that ruled southern Iraq, Kuwait, and Eastern Arabia from the 15th to 18th centuries, with their domain extending from Iraq in the north to the borders of Oman in the south. The Al-Thawadi are documented as being present across the Persian Gulf states of Bahrain, Qatar, and Saudi Arabia, representing one of the sub-clans of this influential tribal confederation.

In Bahrain, the Al-Thawadi established themselves primarily in Al Hidd and Muharraq, where many family members became deeply involved in the region's lucrative pearl hunting industry. The family was among the Arab clans that migrated to Bahrain from Zubarah following the 1783 Bani Utbah invasion of Bahrain, marking a significant chapter in their settlement history. This migration was part of broader population movements that reshaped the demographic landscape of Bahrain during the late 18th century.

The modern Al-Thawadi family has produced notable figures across multiple sectors. In Qatar, Hassan Al-Thawadi emerged as a prominent figure, serving as Secretary General of the Supreme Committee for Delivery and Legacy for the 2022 FIFA World Cup. Born to a Qatari diplomat, Hassan Al-Thawadi is fluent in multiple languages and gained recognition as one of World Soccer magazine's 2022 People of the Year, though his career was shadowed by bribery allegations related to Qatar's World Cup bid. In Bahrain, the family has been particularly prominent in media and finance, with Bassam Mohammed Al-Thawadi becoming a pioneering filmmaker who produced Bahrain's first feature film 'The Barrier' in 1990, and Isa Bin Sultan Al Thawadi founding the Housing Bank. The family also includes political activists like Ahmad Al-Thawadi, known for his leftist political activism.

The Al-Thawadi family's modern presence spans diplomatic, business, and cultural spheres across the Gulf states. Their historical connection to the Bani Khalid confederation links them to one of the most significant tribal powers in Arabian Peninsula history, while their contemporary achievements demonstrate successful adaptation to modern Gulf society and economy.
data.description: The Al-Thawadi is an Arab tribal family originating from the Bani Khalid confederation, with significant presence across Bahrain, Qatar, and Saudi Arabia. The family has produced notable figures in government, diplomacy, media, finance, and sports administration, including Qatar's 2022 World Cup organizing committee leadership.
data.originStory: The Al-Thawadi trace their origins to the Bani Khalid tribe, a major Arab tribal confederation that historically ruled Eastern Arabia from the 15th to 18th centuries. They migrated to Bahrain from Zubarah following the 1783 Bani Utbah invasion, establishing themselves in Al Hidd and Muharraq where they became involved in the pearl hunting industry.
data.modernStatus: The family maintains prominent positions across Gulf states, with Hassan Al-Thawadi having served as Secretary General for Qatar's 2022 World Cup organizing committee, Isa Bin Sultan Al Thawadi as founder of the Housing Bank in Bahrain, and various members active in media, finance, and political activism.

data.history:
The House of Sabah is the ruling family of Kuwait, having governed since 1752 when they were chosen by merchant families to lead the emerging trading settlement. Originating from the Bani Utbah tribal confederation, they trace their lineage to Sabah I bin Jaber, who became the first emir and ruled until his death in 1762. According to historical accounts, the Al Sabah family were expelled from Umm Qasr in southern Iraq by the Ottomans due to their activities involving preying on caravans in Basra and trading ships in the Shatt al-Arab. Prior to settling in Kuwait, they lived across various regions in southern Iran and Iraq. One oral tradition states they fled drought in central Arabia in the early 1700s, migrated south, then moved to Zubara on Qatar's west coast before finally settling in Kuwait around the mid-1700s where they found water.

The dynasty transformed from pearl merchants and traders into leaders of one of the world's wealthiest oil-producing nations. A pivotal figure was Mubarak the Great (1896-1915), known as 'The lion of the peninsula,' who ascended the throne after assassinating his predecessor and half-brother Muhammad Al-Sabah in May 1896. Mubarak forged the critical alliance with Britain through the Anglo-Kuwaiti Treaty of 23 November 1899, pledging himself and his successors not to receive foreign agents or representatives or to cede territory without British approval. This agreement is regarded as foundational to modern Kuwait. The Kuwaiti constitution mandates that the Emir must be a descendant of Mubarak from the ruling Al-Sabah family.

Mubarak was also the father of two rulers who succeeded him, Jaber and Salim, from which the Al-Jaber and Al-Salim branches of the Al-Sabah family originated respectively, and is the paternal ancestor of all successive rulers and prime ministers of Kuwait. Other key historical figures include Sheikh Abdullah Al-Salim who ended British protectorate status in 1961 and introduced Kuwait's Constitution in 1962. The family maintained control through various challenges, including the Gulf War when they operated an exiled government from Saudi Arabia, and continues to hold key government positions today. Notable contemporary figures include Salem Al-Ali Al-Sabah (1926–2024), who served as commander of the Kuwait National Guard from 1967–2024, and Salem Abdulaziz Al Sabah (born 1951), a Kuwaiti royal and politician.
data.description: The House of Sabah is the ruling family of Kuwait, having governed since 1752 and transformed from pearl merchants into leaders of one of the world's wealthiest oil-producing nations. The family continues to hold key government positions with the Kuwaiti constitution mandating that the Emir must be a descendant of Mubarak Al-Sabah.
data.originStory: The Al Sabah branch of the Utub tribe settled in Kuwait in the early 18th century after being expelled from Umm Qasr in southern Iraq by the Ottomans. One oral tradition states they fled drought in central Arabia in the early 1700s, migrated south to Zubara on Qatar's west coast, before finally settling in Kuwait around the mid-1700s where they found water. They were elected as rulers in 1752 by the merchant families and tribal leaders, with Sabah I bin Jaber becoming the first emir.
data.modernStatus: Ruling family of Kuwait holding key government positions including the Emir, Crown Prince, Prime Minister, and various ministerial roles. Controls significant oil wealth and continues dynastic rule through constitutional monarchy.

data.history: The Zilkha family emerged from the ancient Jewish community of Baghdad, where Khedouri (Khuburi) Aboudi Zilkha established a small money-changing house in 1899 that rapidly evolved into a regional financial institution known as Maison de Banque K.A. Zilkha. This represented one of the earliest attempts to create an organized branch banking network in the Middle East. Beginning in the late 1920s, the family expanded regionally by opening branches in Beirut (1927), Damascus (1935), and Cairo (1937), establishing themselves as pioneering international bankers in the Ottoman and post-Ottoman Middle East. However, following the 1948 Arab-Israeli War, the family's assets faced systematic confiscation: first in Iraq in 1950, then in Egypt in 1956 during nationalization policies, and in Syria during the same period. This forced the family to relocate their financial center of gravity to Europe and the United States, where they successfully rebuilt their business empire across multiple sectors including retail, energy, and venture capital. The family's most notable business achievements include Selim Zilkha's founding of the Mothercare retail chain in the UK and his later billion-dollar success in petroleum with Towner Petroleum, while Donald Zilkha established multiple investment firms including Zilkha & Company and became involved in the acquisition of Colt's Manufacturing Company. The family represents part of the broader Iraqi Jewish diaspora that fled the Middle East during the mid-20th century persecution and successfully reestablished themselves in Western financial markets.
data.description: The Zilkha family are prominent Baghdadi Jewish merchants and bankers who built a major financial empire across the Middle East before relocating to the West in the mid-20th century. They remain influential in international business, particularly in energy and finance sectors.
data.originStory: The family emerged from the ancient Jewish community of Baghdad, establishing themselves as leading merchants and bankers in Ottoman Iraq during the late 19th and early 20th centuries.
data.modernStatus: The family maintains significant business interests through various investment firms including Zilkha & Company, Zilkha Venture Partners, and Zilkha Partners L.P., with involvement in private equity, venture capital, and public company investments.

data.history: The research provided does not contain information about the Al Majid merchant family of Bani Yas origin from Abu Dhabi described in the existing data. Instead, the research focuses extensively on the Iraqi Al-Majid family, specifically Ali Hassan al-Majid (c. 1941-2010) and his relatives who were part of Saddam Hussein's inner circle. Ali Hassan al-Majid was born in al-Awja near Tikrit and belonged to the Bejat clan of the Al-Bu Nasir tribe, the same tribal group as Saddam Hussein. He rose from working as a motorcycle messenger in the Iraqi Army to become a powerful military officer and politician under Saddam Hussein's regime. The Iraqi Al-Majid family gained notoriety through their political and military roles during Ba'ath Party rule, particularly Ali Hassan's involvement in the genocidal Al-Anfal campaign against Kurdish populations in the 1980s, which earned him the nickname 'Chemical Ali.' The research also mentions other Al-Majid family members including Hassan Abd al-Majid (Saddam's father's brother), who had sons including Ali Hassan al-Majid, Hisham (Special Republican Guard Commander), and Kamel al-Majid. The family was deeply involved in Iraq's security apparatus and military operations. Hussein Kamel al-Majid and Saddam Kamel al-Majid, sons of Kamel, were married to Saddam Hussein's daughters but defected to Jordan in 1995, only to return in 1996 and be killed shortly after. The research provides no connection between this Iraqi political/military Al-Majid family and any UAE merchant family, nor does it support the existence of a Bani Yas Al Majid merchant family in Abu Dhabi.
data.description: Based on the available research, no verified information exists about an Al Majid merchant family of Bani Yas origin in Abu Dhabi. The research exclusively covers the Iraqi Al-Majid family associated with Saddam Hussein's regime.

data.history: The Al Mansoori family represents a prominent merchant lineage of Bani Yas tribal origin from the Liwa Oasis. According to existing family records, they migrated to Abu Dhabi in the early 1800s where they established themselves as successful pearl traders and merchants. The research reveals notable modern descendants including Hazzaa Al Mansoori (born 1983), the UAE's first astronaut who flew to the International Space Station in 2019, becoming the first Emirati and Arab from the Gulf region to reach space. His full name is Hazzaa Ali Abdan Khalfan Al Mansoori, and he was born in Al Wathba, Abu Dhabi, spending his childhood in the Liwa desert region where his family originated. Another prominent family member is Dr. Hawaa Al Thahak Al Mansoori, an accomplished physician and politician who serves on the Federal National Council of the UAE since 2019. She was the first Emirati woman to receive a UAE Presidential Scholarship to study medicine in the United States, completing her education at the University of Maryland and George Washington University. The family has maintained strong connections to their ancestral Liwa region while achieving prominence in modern UAE society across space exploration, medicine, politics, and government service. Historical connections are also evident through marriage alliances, as Sheikh Zayed bin Khalifa Al Nahyan (1835-1909), known as 'Zayed the Great' and ruler of Abu Dhabi for 54 years, had a wife named Maitha bint Salmeen Bourhama Al Mansouri, indicating long-standing ties between the Al Mansoori family and the ruling Al Nahyan dynasty.
data.description: The Al Mansoori family is a prominent merchant family of Bani Yas tribal origin, originally from the Liwa Oasis, who migrated to Abu Dhabi in the early 19th century. They have achieved modern prominence through space exploration, medicine, and politics, with members serving in high government positions and representing the UAE internationally.

data.modernStatus: The family has achieved prominence in multiple sectors: aerospace (Hazzaa Al Mansoori as UAE's first astronaut), medicine and politics (Dr. Hawaa Al Thahak Al Mansoori as Federal National Council member), and continued involvement in government service.

data.history: The Al Rajhi family traces their origins to the Aniza tribe in Al-Bukayriyah, Al-Qassim Province, where they began as currency exchangers and traders serving Hajj pilgrims in the early 20th century. The family's transformation from Najdi desert traders to global banking dynasty began with Sheikh Saleh Abdul Aziz Al Rajhi (1921-2011), whose father Abdul-Aziz Al-Rajhi was a farmer and trader of lesser means who moved the family from Al-Bukayriyah to Riyadh seeking better business prospects. In the early 1930s, young Saleh worked odd jobs in local marketplaces, initially as a porter before establishing a bastah (small trading place) in 1937 in front of the Grand Mosque in Riyadh, changing money for a 5% commission. By 1947, Saleh opened a formal currency shop in Riyadh and brought his brothers Mohammed and Sulaiman into the business, with Sulaiman expanding operations to Mecca and Jeddah to serve pilgrims and businesspeople. The brothers formally established the beginnings of Al-Rajhi Bank in 1957, connecting to international banking networks across Europe, Asia, and the United States by the 1960s. In 1983, they won permission to open Saudi Arabia's first Islamic bank, capitalizing on the flood of migrant workers during the 1970s oil boom who needed to send earnings home to places like India and Pakistan. The family's business empire expanded beyond banking into agriculture, real estate, construction, and other industrial sectors. Sheikh Sulaiman bin Abdulaziz Al Rajhi became chairman of what is recognized as the Tadawul's most venerable institution, with his wealth estimated at $7.7 billion by Forbes in 2011, making him the 120th richest person in the world. The family continues as Al Rajhi Bank's majority shareholders and remains among Saudi Arabia's wealthiest non-royals and leading philanthropists, with Sulaiman establishing Sulaiman Al Rajhi University and announcing in 2011 his intention to donate most of his fortune to charity.
data.description: The Al Rajhi family is Saudi Arabia's most prominent banking dynasty, having transformed from Najdi desert traders into the founders of the world's largest Islamic bank. Their business empire, built on strict Islamic principles, has made them one of the wealthiest families in the Middle East.

Their reputation for honesty and adherence to Islamic principles laid the foundation for what would become a global banking empire.

data.history: The Al Fardan family is a prominent Qatari business dynasty with documented Persian/Iranian origins, contrary to the previously claimed Bani Yas tribal confederation background. The family's business empire was established in 1954 when Ibrahim Alfardan founded the Alfardan Group, creating one of Qatar's major conglomerates with interests spanning multiple sectors including automotive, real estate, hospitality, and financial services across the GCC. The family's contemporary prominence is exemplified by Dana Al Fardan, born in 1985 as the daughter of Ali Al Fardan and Shafiqa Habib, who represents the modern face of this merchant dynasty. Initially working in the family business after obtaining degrees in international relations and gemology from the American University of Sharjah and the Gemological Institute of America in London respectively, Dana later transitioned to become Qatar's first female contemporary composer and cultural pioneer. Her cultural influence extends through her groundbreaking work as a composer, her role as cultural ambassador for the Qatar Philharmonic Orchestra, and her establishment of DNA Records in 2015 as Qatar's first record label. The family demonstrates continued regional engagement through philanthropic work with organizations like the Qatar Red Crescent Society and UNRWA, showing their involvement in humanitarian causes. The Al Fardan name also appears in Bahraini contexts, with racing driver Hamad Al Fardan (son of rally driver Ahmed Al Fardan) and journalist Hani Al-Fardan, though specific family connections require further verification.

data.history:
The Al Gaz family's documented history centers around Mohamed Abdallah AlGaz (c. 1920-2016), who established the family's prominence in Dubai's business and political circles. Born in Al Dhagaya, Deira around 1920 to Abdulla Mohamed AlFalasi, the family belongs to the Al Falasi tribe, the same tribal lineage as Dubai's ruling Al Maktoum family. Mohamed's father died when he was an adolescent, leaving the family in modest circumstances. During World War II's famine in Dubai, the family temporarily relocated to Bahrain while Mohamed found work as a carpenter in Dammam, Saudi Arabia, using his earnings to establish commodity trading operations between Bahrain, Kuwait, and Dubai.

In the 1950s, Mohamed formed a historic business partnership with Juma Al Majid, a grocer who became his lifelong business associate and friend. Together they established the famous 'Mohamed & Juma Al Majid' company, which became central to the Switzerland-Dubai-India gold trade and secured exclusive distribution rights for major international brands including General Electric, Samsung, Phillips, Opel, Pepsi, Nissan, Hyundai, and Kia. After eleven successful years, they amicably split their business empire, with Mohamed focusing on real estate development while maintaining their friendship and philanthropic partnerships.

Following the business split, Mohamed concentrated on real estate development, building Dubai's first three-story building and developing significant areas around Port Said, including the iconic clock-tower plaza and Hamriya in Bur Deira. Post-UAE federation in 1971, he transitioned into public service, serving as the first Deputy Chairman of the Central Bank of the UAE from 1980 through the mid-1990s under Sheikh Suroor bin Mohammad Al Nahyan. He had previously been nominated to the Dubai Municipal Council on July 7, 1958, where he served on the budget and treasury committee and acted as a diplomatic adviser to Sheikh Rashid bin Saeed al Maktoum.

Mohamed became a pioneering philanthropist in the UAE, co-founding the country's first charitable society, Beit Al Khair, in 1989 with his former business partner Juma Al Majid. He built several schools including the Amna bint Wahb Girls School and Gamal Abdulnasser Boys School, and made numerous anonymous donations to public and private institutions. When he died on April 12, 2016, at age 96, his funeral was attended by UAE President Sheikh Mohammed bin Zayed Al Nahyan and Prime Minister Sheikh Mohammed bin Rashid Al Maktoum, reflecting his significant contributions to the UAE's development. He was posthumously honored with the UAE Pioneers Award in 2018 for his contributions to the humanitarian sector.

data.modernStatus: The family's modern legacy is primarily through Mohamed Abdallah AlGaz's historical contributions to UAE banking, real estate development, and philanthropy, with his work in establishing Beit Al Khair charity continuing as a major UAE charitable institution.

data.history: The Alireza name encompasses multiple distinct lineages across the Middle East, most prominently the House of Alireza merchant family and various Iranian political and religious figures. The House of Alireza, founded in 1845, represents Saudi Arabia's oldest organized commercial enterprise, established by Zainal Alireza after his arrival in Jeddah during the 1840s from Laristan in southern Iran. This Persian merchant family initially traded food, textiles, and merchandise from the Middle East and Indian subcontinent, while serving as hajj agents for British-Indian shipping lines. The family became early political supporters of Abdulaziz al Saud, with members like Qasim bin Zainal Alireza elected as deputy for Jeddah in the Ottoman parliament of 1908, and Abdullah Alireza Zainal serving as kaymakam (governor) of Jeddah during Hashemite and early Saudi rule. The business expanded significantly, acquiring the first Ford dealership in Saudi Arabia in 1940 and rights to Western brands like Pepsi Cola and Goodyear. In 1929, the business was renamed Haji Abdullah Alireza & Co. and received Saudi Arabia's first commercial registration (license number 1), eventually incorporating as a limited liability company in 2003. Separate from the merchant family, the Alireza name appears prominently in Iranian political and religious circles, including Ali Reza Pahlavi (1966-2011), the younger son of the last Shah Mohammad Reza Pahlavi, who studied ancient Iranian studies at Harvard before his death by suicide in Boston. Contemporary figures include Alireza Arafi, born 1959 in Maybod to a Lak-Kurdish family whose ancestors converted from Zoroastrianism to Islam in the 19th century, who has served as head of Iran's seminaries since 2016 and was appointed to Iran's Guardian Council in 2019.

Other Alireza lineages have different origins, including Iranian royal family members and clerics with various regional backgrounds including Lak-Kurdish ancestry.
data.modernStatus: The House of Alireza operates as a diversified business empire including companies like Xenel, with descendants serving as executives. Contemporary Iranian Alireza figures hold positions in religious and political institutions, with Alireza Arafi serving on Iran's Guardian Council and as head of national seminaries.

data.history: Based on the research provided, the Al Jeraisy family appears to have political connections in the Nazareth area of northern Israel. The only documented reference to the family name appears in connection with Ramez Jeraisy, who served as Mayor of Nazareth and worked alongside Israeli industrialist Stef Wertheimer on economic development projects. In 2009, Mayor Ramez Jeraisy met with Pope Benedict XVI at the site of a planned industrial park in Nazareth, where the Pope gave his blessing to the coexistence project. The industrial park, which opened in April 2013, was described as part of a unique model to promote the advancement of Arab-Jewish Israeli export companies, with Jews and Arabs working side by side. Wertheimer and Jeraisy explained that the industrial park represented a practical approach to coexistence through economic cooperation. However, the research documents do not provide information about the family's origins, tribal connections, business activities beyond municipal governance, or broader historical role. The family's ethnic background, migration history, and genealogical connections remain undocumented in the available sources.

data.history: Al-Emadi (Arabic: العمادي) is an Arab family and tribal lineage commonly found in Qatar and Bahrain. The family has produced numerous prominent businessmen, diplomats, politicians, and government officials in Qatar over several decades. The House of Thani was established in Qatar in 1825 with Sheikh Mohammed bin Thani as the first leader, during which period many influential families like Al-Emadi became established in the region's political and economic landscape. The family gained particular prominence in Qatar's modern banking and government sectors, with members serving in key ministerial positions and leading major financial institutions. Most notably, Ali Sharif Al Emadi served as Qatar's Minister of Finance from June 2013 to May 2021, succeeding Yousef Hussain Kamal in the post following Sheikh Tamim bin Hamad Al Thani's ascension as Emir. Al Emadi was also chairman of Qatar National Bank (QNB Group) and served on the administrative council of the Qatar Investment Authority. However, his career ended in controversy when he was arrested in May 2021 on charges of misuse of public money, abuse of function and power, and in March 2023 was charged with bribery, abuse of position and power, damage to public funds and money laundering. The family continues to have members in various sectors including the Shura Council, with Khalid Kamal Al-Emadi and Ismail Shareef Al-Emadi serving as council members, and in diplomatic roles with Hassan bin Mohammed Rafee Al-Emadi serving as ambassador and politician.
data.description: An Arab family and tribal lineage prominent in Qatar and Bahrain, known for producing influential businessmen, government ministers, ambassadors, and banking executives, though recently marked by the corruption charges against former Finance Minister Ali Sharif Al Emadi.
data.modernStatus: The family maintains influence in Qatar's government and business sectors, with members serving in the Shura Council and diplomatic positions. However, their prominence was significantly affected by the arrest and corruption charges against Ali Sharif Al Emadi, former Minister of Finance and QNB Group chairman, who was dismissed from his banking position following his May 2021 arrest.

data.history: The research reveals multiple unrelated lineages using variations of the Towell name across different regions and time periods. The O'Toole family represents an ancient Irish clan (Ó Tuathail) descended from Tuathal Mac Augaire, King of Leinster (died 958), originally controlling territory in County Kildare before being driven to County Wicklow during the Anglo-Norman invasion. The name variants include Towell, Towle, and Tool among others. In modern times, various unrelated individuals have borne Towell surnames, including Scottish boxer Mike Towell (1991-2016), Canadian photographer Larry Towell (born 1953), and American politician David Towell (1937-2003) who served as a U.S. representative from Nevada. In the Gulf region, specifically Oman, a significant business partnership existed between Hajj Moosa AbdulRahman Hassan and W.J. Towell & Co., forming part of the Gary McKenzie Moosa Towell Group of Companies in 1956, which later became Oman United Agencies. This company provided supplies and logistics for oil companies in Oman and had divisions for foodstuffs and travel services. However, the research provides no information connecting any GCC Towell family to the Irish O'Toole lineage, establishing their ethnic origins, migration patterns, or founding story in the Gulf region. The business connection appears to be a commercial partnership rather than indicating a Towell family presence in the region.
data.description: Multiple unrelated lineages using Towell variants, including ancient Irish clan O'Toole and business partnerships in Oman, but no verified connection between them or confirmed GCC family lineage.
data.originStory: Origins disputed - multiple unrelated lineages exist using Towell variants. Irish O'Toole lineage traces to ancient Leinster kings, but no verified connection to any Gulf-based Towell family has been established.
data.modernStatus: No confirmed modern GCC-based Towell family status identified in research. Historical business partnership with Omani entities through W.J. Towell & Co. in mid-20th century.
data.nameEtymology: Irish variants derive from Ó Tuathail, with multiple related forms including O'Toole, Toole, Tool, Tooley, Toal, Toale, Tohill, Toohill, Towle, Towell, and others

data.history: The research provided reveals a critical error in the existing tribal genealogy database. The Afar referenced as a sub-tribe of the Arabian Peninsula's Awamir confederation is actually the Afar people (Qafár/Danakil) of the Horn of Africa, who are a Cushitic ethnic group, not an Arab tribe. The Afar people inhabit the Afar Region of Ethiopia, northern Djibouti, and the entire southern coast of Eritrea, with their traditional territories bordering both the Red Sea and the Gulf of Aden. They speak the Afar language, which belongs to the East Cushitic branch of the Afroasiatic family, making them linguistically distinct from Arabic-speaking tribes. The earliest surviving written mention of the Afar dates to the 13th century by Andalusian writer Ibn Sa'id, who reported of a people called Dankal inhabiting an area extending from the port of Suakin to as far south as Mandeb, near Zeila. The Afar have been consistently mentioned in Ethiopian records since the royal chronicles of Emperor Amda Seyon, where they were described as inhabiting the area known as 'Adal' from the lower Awash River to north of Lake Abbe. Afar society has traditionally been organized into independent kingdoms ruled by sultans, including the Sultanate of Aussa, Sultanate of Tadjourah, Sultanate of Rahaito, and others. They are divided into two main subgroups: the Asaimara ('Red Men') who are considered nobility, and the Adoimara ('White Men') who form the working class. The Afar have been predominantly Muslim since the 13th century and are primarily pastoralists raising camels, goats, sheep, and cattle. This entry represents a fundamental misidentification and requires complete removal from Arabian Peninsula tribal genealogy records, as the Afar are definitively not an Arab tribal group from the Arabian Peninsula but rather a distinct Cushitic people of the Horn of Africa.
data.nameEtymology: The etymology of 'Dankali' can be traced back to the Afar language, derived from 'dan' (meaning 'people' or 'nation') and 'kali' (referring to the Afar Region)

Al'asead is documented as one of the Palestinian branches of the Mahamid tribe (al-Maḥāmīd), a major Arab tribal confederation that traces its genealogical origins to the Khawlaniyah al-Qahtaniyah Harb, placing them within the Qahtanite Arab lineage. The Mahamid originally resided in Yemen and the Hijaz region between Mecca and Medina, establishing themselves as part of the broader Harb tribal confederation that dominated these territories in the pre-Islamic and early Islamic periods.

According to tribal genealogies, when the Mahamid territories in Yemen, Najd, and Hijaz became overcrowded, the tribe faced a critical decision. Rather than engaging in warfare with neighboring Harb clans - many of whom were related sub-clans united by common fifth-generation ancestors - the Mahamid chose collective migration as their solution. This decision reflects the complex inter-tribal relationships and the preference for maintaining kinship bonds over territorial conquest that characterized Arabian tribal politics.

The Mahamid migration followed a northern route, first moving to Dumat Al-Jandal in northern Arabia, where they settled temporarily before continuing their journey toward the Levant. The tribe eventually reached the Karak region in present-day Jordan, where their numbers were so substantial that they outnumbered the largest existing tribes in the area. They formed strategic alliances with local Karak clans, most notably the Al-Omaro Bani Uqba, and established the Emirate of Mahamid under the leadership of the Al-Amru clan, which maintained control for an extended period.

Al'asead emerged as one of the distinct Palestinian branches of this larger Mahamid confederation, alongside other documented branches including alkhudur, alsawalima, alhamamada, almusaeada, alhasasana, aljeayisa, dar alearabi, and al'ahmad. The specific circumstances of Al'asead's settlement in Palestine, their territorial distribution, and their relationships with other Palestinian Mahamid branches are not detailed in available genealogical records, though their inclusion in the comprehensive list of Palestinian Mahamid branches indicates their established presence and recognized status within the tribal confederation's Palestinian territories.

data.history: No verified historical information about Al Bu Kharaiban is available in the provided research materials. The tribe's origins, genealogical connections, territorial base, and historical development remain undocumented and require further scholarly investigation.

The research provided does not contain information about the Al Nasrallah tribe as a distinct Arabian Peninsula tribal entity. Instead, the research focuses on Abdul-Malik al-Houthi, who belongs to the Houthi tribe of northern Yemen's Saada Governorate. The Houthi tribe, from which Abdul-Malik hails, is part of Yemen's Zaydi Shia Muslim community and represents a different tribal lineage entirely from any Al Nasrallah grouping. The existing data mentions various Al Nasrallah families across the Arabian Peninsula and Gulf states, including connections to the Anazzah tribe in Kuwait and Alid families in Karbala, but the current research does not provide verification or additional details about these specific tribal connections. Without additional research materials that directly address the Al Nasrallah tribal genealogy, migrations, or historical events, it is not possible to provide a comprehensive tribal history based solely on the Houthi-related research provided.

No research was provided to support any historical claims about Al al-Tha'alib (Tha'laba). While existing data suggests connections to the Tayy tribal confederation and Qahtani lineage, without research documentation, these claims cannot be verified or expanded upon.

The Almatiei are one of the main branches of the Mahamid tribe (Arabic: المحاميد), which traces its lineage to the Khawlaniyah al-Qahtaniyah Harb tribal confederation. The Mahamid originally resided in Yemen and the Hijaz region between Mecca and Medina before undergoing significant migrations that would scatter the tribe across the Arabian Peninsula and beyond. According to tribal accounts, when the Mahamid population grew large and their traditional territories in Yemen, Najd, and Hijaz became insufficient to support them, the tribal leadership convened to determine their course of action. When some suggested conquering neighboring territories, this proposal was rejected because most surrounding tribes were clans and sub-clans of the Harb confederation, making such conflicts inadvisable. Instead, those united by the fifth grandfather chose the path of migration, making a collective decision to leave their ancestral lands. The tribe's migration took them northward to Dumat Al-Jandal, where they settled temporarily before continuing their journey toward the Levant. Eventually reaching the Karak region, the Mahamid established themselves in such numbers that they outnumbered the largest local tribes and formed alliances with Karak clans, most notably with Al-Omaro Bani Uqba. Under the leadership of the Al-Amru clan, they established the Emirate of Mahamid, which maintained control over the region for an extended period. However, historical sources and Orientalist accounts suggest their rule was characterized by harshness toward those under their authority, eventually prompting various local clans to unite against Mahamid rule. Today, the Almatiei remain one of the recognized branches of the Mahamid in Saudi Arabia, alongside other branches including alrtuei, almadhakir, almuhabida, and alrathea, maintaining their distinct tribal identity within the broader Mahamid confederation that has spread across the Arabian Peninsula, Levant, Iraq, and North Africa.

data.history: The Anaza confederation represents one of the most significant Bedouin tribal movements in Arabian history, with documented existence predating Islam in the 7th century. Classical Arab genealogists placed Anizah within the large Rabi'a branch of the Adnanites, alongside tribes like Abd al-Qays, Banu Bakr, Bani Hanifa, and Taghlib. Originally, two main branches existed: nomadic groups in the northern Arabian steppes bordering Syria and Mesopotamia, and sedentary clans in the wadis of Al-Yamama district in eastern Najd. The Anizah participated in the Battle of Siffin supporting Ali ibn Abi Talib, contributing 4,000 armored warriors. According to historians al-Hamdani and Abu'l-Hasan Bayhaqi, the tribe settled in Khaybar at the end of the 10th century AD, expelling the descendants of Ja'far al-Tayyar from the region. During the Ottoman era, Anizah became masters of northwestern Arabian oasis towns, particularly Khaybar and Al-'Ula, levying crops from inhabitants while migrating seasonally to southern Syria where they collected khuwwa tribute from Hawran region farmers. The first Anizah subtribe to enter Syria was the Hasana in the mid-18th century, establishing dominance around Hama and Homs. The Wuld Ali entered Hawran simultaneously, followed by the Ruwallah in the early 19th century, while the Fad'an and Sba'a settled in central Syrian plains. The Ruwallah branch became so powerful they practically developed into an independent tribe, engaging in battles with other Anizah branches and becoming arch-enemies of the Shammar tribe. In Nejd, 19th-century chronicles record Anizah's penetration into northern and western regions, with major migration into Al-Qassim region in the 15th century. By the early 18th century, Anizah Bedouins reached Riyadh's gates, killing ruler Zayd Abazara'a in battle. Under Sheikh Mashaan Ibn Hathal's leadership, Anizah unified against tribal enemies at the Battle of Al Shimasiyah on June 18, 1825. The establishment of modern Middle Eastern borders in the early 20th century severely disrupted the Bedouin lifestyle, forcing most Anizah members to settle within new nation-states.

data.history: Based on the research provided, there is limited specific information about the Bani Saad tribal confederation mentioned in the existing data. The research confirms the existence of Khan Bani Saad, a majority Shia Arab city in Diyala Governorate, Iraq, which suggests some connection to a Bani Saad tribal presence in that region. However, the research does not provide detailed historical information about the specific Bani Saad confederation referenced in the existing data. The research does mention the broader context of Arabian tribal genealogy, noting the division between Qahtani and Adnani lineages, with most genealogists classifying Arab peoples into these two main categories based on their origins in Yemen (Qahtani) or Hijaz (Adnani). Within the Harb tribe structure documented in the research, there is a mention of 'Alsawaid (Al-Saadi)' among the Masrooh branch, which may represent a different Saad-related group but cannot be definitively connected to the Bani Saad confederation without additional evidence. The research indicates that tribal affiliations and movements were complex throughout Arabian history, with various groups sharing similar names but having distinct origins and territories. Without more specific documentation about the particular Bani Saad confederation in question, the historical narrative must remain limited to what can be directly supported by the available research materials.

data.history:
The Banu Aws was one of the two major Arab tribes of Medina (ancient Yathrib) during the pre-Islamic and early Islamic periods, alongside their rivals the Banu Khazraj. Both tribes descended from Thaʻlaba bin ʻAmr, son of King Muzayqiya who ruled parts of Yemen in the 2nd century and led the Azd tribe. The Banu Aws trace their lineage to the Qahtani (South Arabian) tribal confederation, having migrated from Yemen as part of the pre-Islamic exodus following damage to the Great Marib Dam around the 2nd century CE according to Abu Muhammad Al-Hasan Ibn Ahmad Al-Hamdani.

Around 300 CE, Thaʻlaba bin ʻAmr separated from his tribe and settled in Yathrib, which was then controlled by three major Jewish tribes: Banu Nadir, Banu Qurayza, and Banu Qaynuqa. Initially, both the Banu Aws and Banu Khazraj (collectively known as Banu Qayla) were subordinate to these Jewish clans. However, around the 5th century, they gained independence when Mālik bin Ajlān of Khazraj asserted control, allowing both Arab tribes to obtain shares of palm groves and strongholds in the oasis.

The Banu Aws engaged in prolonged conflicts with their cousin tribe Banu Khazraj for over a century, with tensions escalating into open warfare. During these conflicts, the Jewish tribes of Medina aligned with different sides - the Banu Aws allied with the Jewish tribes Banu Nadir and Banu Qurayza, while Banu Khazraj allied with Banu Qaynuqa. The final and most decisive battle was the Battle of Bu'ath around 617 CE, where the Banu Aws achieved victory over the Banu Khazraj despite initial setbacks.

After Muhammad's arrival in Medina in 622 CE following the Hijrah, both the Banu Aws and Banu Khazraj converted to Islam and became known as the Ansar (helpers). They were included in the Constitution of Medina as allies to the Muslims, being described as 'one nation/community with the Believers.' The tribe played crucial roles in early Islamic history, including the assassination of Ka'b ibn al-Ashraf (carried out by Banu Aws members) and participation in various military expeditions under Muhammad's leadership. Notable Aws leaders included Sa'ad ibn Mua'dh, who later served as arbitrator to decide the fate of Banu Qurayza after their siege in 627 CE, and Bashir ibn Sa'ad, both of whom held positions of significant influence in the early Muslim community.

data.history:
Banu Lahyan was an ancient Arabian tribe that established a significant kingdom in the Hijaz region, particularly around Dedan (modern Al-Ula in Saudi Arabia). They controlled important trade routes between Yemen and the Levant during the pre-Islamic period, reaching their peak between the 5th-6th centuries CE. The tribe is known for their Lihyanite inscriptions found in northwest Arabian Peninsula, representing one of the notable pre-Islamic kingdoms in the region.

During the early Islamic period, the tribe came into direct conflict with the Muslim community in Medina. In 625 CE, during the Expedition of Abdullah Ibn Unais, Prophet Muhammad sent Abdullah ibn Unais to assassinate Khaled bin Sufyan Al-Hathali, the chief of Banu Lahyan, alleging that he was planning to attack Medina and incite the people of Nakhla and Uranah against the Muslims. The tensions escalated following the Expedition of Al Raji, where 10 Muslims were killed, an incident for which Muhammad held the Banu Lahyan responsible.

In September 627 CE (Rabi' al-awwal or Jumada Al-Awwal, 6 AH), Muhammad launched a direct military campaign against the tribe, leading 200 Muslim fighters in what became known as the Invasion of Banu Lahyan. Muhammad wanted to get justice for the killing of 10 Muslims in the Expedition of Al Raji. The Banu Lahyan were situated deep in the heart of Hijaz on the borders of Mecca, and due to deep-seated blood-revenge between the Muslims and the Arabian tribes, Muhammad seized the opportunity when the power of the allied Confederates collapsed.

The tribe, having received advance warning of the approaching Muslim force, fled to the mountain tops and remained out of Muhammad's reach. Muhammad set out with 200 Muslim fighters, making a feint of heading for Syria, then changing route towards Batn Gharran, the scene where 10 Muslims were killed in the Expedition of Al Raji. The tribe's strategic retreat demonstrated their knowledge of the local terrain and their determination to avoid subjugation to the expanding Islamic state. The campaign lasted 14 days before Muhammad's forces returned to Medina, with the tribe successfully evading the Muslim army through their tactical withdrawal to higher ground.

data.history:
Banu Quda'a was a major pre-Islamic tribal confederation spanning the Arabian Peninsula and Levant, with disputed genealogical origins - some claiming descent from Ma'add (making them Adnani/northern Arabian), others from Himyar (making them Qahtani/southern Arabian). The confederation included numerous constituent tribes, with the Banu Kalb emerging as its largest and most politically significant component in northern territories, while other major branches included the Tayy, Banu Udhra, and various smaller tribes across different regions.

The Banu Kalb, as the confederation's most prominent branch, dominated northwestern Arabia and central Syria from their base in al-Jawf and Wadi Sirhan, gradually expanding into Byzantine Syria where they served as foederati from the 4th century CE. They embraced Monophysite Christianity under Ghassanid authority and became skilled in military discipline. The Tayy branch migrated from Yemen to northern Arabia in the 2nd century CE, settling in the Shammar and Salma mountain ranges (later called Jabal Tayy), where they split into two main divisions: Al al-Ghawth and Al Jadila, the latter converting to Christianity and migrating to Syria during the 6th-century Fasad War.

During the Islamic conquests (630s-640s), different Quda'a tribes responded variably to Muslim expansion. The Banu Udhra converted early through a delegation to Prophet Muhammad in 630, while the Kalb initially remained neutral before converting after the Battle of Yarmouk (636). The Tayy under chiefs Adi ibn Hatim and Zayd al-Khayr converted in 629-630 and became companions of the Prophet. Under the Umayyad Caliphate (661-750), the Kalb became the military foundation of the dynasty through strategic marriages with the ruling family, while the Tayy supported Ali during the First Civil War, creating lasting tribal divisions.

By the Abbasid period (750-1258), the confederation's political cohesion had largely dissolved. The Kalb lost influence under pro-Qaysite policies and eventually allied with various movements including the Qarmatians and later the Fatimids, gradually transitioning from nomadism to settled agriculture. The Tayy expanded across the Syrian steppe and northern Arabia, with branches like the Al Fadl and Al Mira controlling vast territories from Homs to central Najd. The Banu Udhra, meanwhile, migrated to Syria, Egypt, and eventually Muslim Spain, settling in Jaén, Almería, and around Algeciras, but retained primarily historical rather than political significance after the early Islamic period.

folkLegends.title: Quda'a's Disputed Ancestry
folkLegends.story: According to Arab genealogical tradition, Quda'a was said to be the son of Ma'add and brother to Nizar, though some genealogists disputed whether he was Qahtani or Adnani. The confederation formed around tribes claiming descent from his various sons.

folkLegends.title: The Naming of Tayy
folkLegends.story: The tribe's progenitor Julhumah ibn Udad received the surname 'Tayy' or 'Tayyi' from the word ṭawā, meaning 'to plaster' in Arabic, because he was said to have been the first to have plastered the walls of a well.

folkLegends.title: Kalb's Animal-Named Ancestry
folkLegends.story: The progenitor Kalb's mother, Asma bint Duraym, was known as Umm al-Asbu (mother of wild animals) because all of her children were named after wild animals, with Kalb meaning 'dog' in Arabic.

data.nameEtymology: Quda'a derives from the name of the confederation's legendary progenitor. The constituent tribe Kalb means 'dog' in Arabic, while Tayy comes from ṭawā meaning 'to plaster', though this etymology is disputed among historians.

data.history:
Banu Thaqif is an Arab tribe tracing its genealogical origins to Qasi ibn Munabbih ibn Bakr ibn Hawazin, whose epithet was 'Thaqif', making them a branch of the larger Hawazin tribal confederation within the Qays group. Unlike their nomadic Hawazin kinsmen, the Thaqif were a settled urban tribe who established themselves in the mountainous city of Ta'if in the Hijaz region, where they built walls around their settlement. The tribe's genealogical claims evolved over time - they initially claimed descent from the Iyad when living on Ta'if's outskirts, but later adopted Hawazin lineage to secure alliance with the nomadic Banu Amir, who had driven out the dominant Adwan tribe from Ta'if. The Thaqif were divided into two main sections: the more prestigious Banu Malik (also called Banu Hutayt), consisting of the Malik ibn Hutayt clan of the Jusham branch, and the Ahlaf ('Allies'), which consisted of the rest of the Jusham branch and all of the Awf branch.

The tribe prospered through multiple economic activities: cultivating Ta'if's renowned orchards and agricultural lands, hosting pilgrims visiting the idol al-Lat housed in their city, and engaging in caravan trade. They both cooperated and competed with the Quraysh of Mecca in trade ventures and agricultural estates, forming considerable marital ties with the influential Umayyad house. The Thaqif initially opposed Islam, contributing forces to the Quraysh against Muhammad at the Battle of Badr in 624 CE and later allying with the Hawazin confederation under Malik ibn Awf al-Nasri against the emerging Muslim state at the Battle of Hunayn in 630 CE. After their defeat and the subsequent siege of Ta'if, a delegation led by chief Abd Yalil negotiated the tribe's conversion to Islam, though they lost their religious prestige as guardians of al-Lat.

Despite their initial opposition, the Thaqif quickly became integral to the Muslim state's expansion due to their literacy, political acumen, and tribal networks, making them indispensable administrators particularly in Iraq and the eastern provinces. They took on crucial leadership roles in the conquest and administration of Iraq, with Thaqafites founding Basra around 638 CE and providing key governors including al-Mughira ibn Shu'ba (governor of Kufa 642-645, 661-671), Ziyad ibn Abihi (governor of Basra and later all Iraq 665-673), and the famous al-Hajjaj ibn Yusuf (governor of Iraq and the east 694-714). Their extensive marital connections with the Quraysh, especially the Umayyads, solidified their position as what historian Hugh Kennedy termed 'a Thaqafi mafia' controlling the eastern Caliphate. Notable Thaqafite commanders included Uthman ibn Abi al-As, who led the first Muslim naval expeditions in the 630s, and Muhammad ibn al-Qasim, the conqueror of Sind in the 710s.

In modern times, during Johann Ludwig Burckhardt's travels to Arabia including Ta'if, he noted that the Thaqif remained 'a very powerful tribe' which controlled most of Ta'if's gardens and agricultural lands, as well as areas along the eastern ridges of the Hejaz mountains. They constituted half of Ta'if's inhabitants at that time, while part of the tribe lived as Bedouins outside the city where they possessed large herds of goats and sheep. Militarily, they lacked horses and camels but could mobilize some two thousand riflemen equipped with matchlocks. Members of the Thaqif, both settled and nomadic, continue to reside in Ta'if and its environs in modern Saudi Arabia.

data.history:
The Chegini (Kurdish: چەگینی Çengînî; Persian: چگنی) are a mostly sedentary Kurdish tribe residing primarily in Iran's Lorestan province, with scattered populations in Qazvin and Fars provinces, as well as in Iraq's Kurdistan Region and Kirkuk. Charles MacGregor classified them as Kurdish in his 1872 topographical work, though the Encyclopædia Iranica notes that some non-Kurdish tribes in Iran share the same name, including a clan of the Amala tribe called Čegīnī in the Qašqāʾī tribal confederacy and the Jabbāra Arabs of Fārs known as Awlād-e Čegīnī. The tribe's origins remain obscure, having migrated to Lorestan from Northwestern Iran at an unspecified time.

During the reign of Tahmasp I (1524-1576), the Chegini Kurds settled in Khorasan while traveling to India, where they prospered under royal protection. By 1597, Sharafkhan Bidlisi documented that the Chegini lived by brigandage and highway robbery, notably lacking any emir or mirza to lead them—an unusual characteristic among Kurdish tribes of that era.

In the early 20th century during Reza Shah's conquest of Lorestan, the Chegini played a significant role in tribal resistance. They successfully fought against and defeated government forces, looting army supplies. When General Shah Bakhti sent his own forces to the region, the Chegini defeated his army decisively, killing around 120 of his men and forcing his retreat. This victory encouraged other tribes to join the resistance.

In late May 1924, the Chegini joined a unified tribal alliance with the Beiranvand, Papi, Baharvand, and half the Judaki tribe to attack Khorramabad, besieging the city for 38 days and driving the army into the city's fortresses. However, by June 1924, disputes over captured weapons and provisions led to the alliance's collapse, with the Chegini withdrawing from the rebellion along with other tribes. Today, the Chegini are among the Kurdish Lak subtribes populating northern Lorestan, particularly in Delfan, Dowreh, and Selseleh counties, where Kurds constitute over 65% of the regional population.

data.history:
Dhu'l-Kala was an ancient Qahtani noble family from the Himyarite kingdom of Yemen, named after their ancestor who held the title 'Dhu'l-Kala' meaning 'possessor of the fortress.' They were among the most powerful aristocratic houses in pre-Islamic South Arabia, with their influence centered in the Himyarite territories of Yemen rather than the northern Arabian Peninsula. The family's prominence peaked under Dhu al-Kala Samayfa ibn Nakur al-Himyari, who led the Dhu al-Kala as the most powerful clan in Yemen during the time of Prophet Muhammad and Caliph Abu Bakr (632-634).

During the Muslim conquest of Syria in the 630s, Samayfa participated as a commander and, like many other Yemenite tribesmen, settled in Homs after its capture in 637. There he became one of the two principal leaders of the Himyar tribe alongside Dhu al-Asbah Kurayb ibn Abraha, with Samayfa being referred to by early Muslim sources as the 'king of Himyar.' According to historian Wilferd Madelung, Samayfa originally intended to settle in Damascus to establish Himyarite dominance over Syria, which conflicted with Qurayshite interests. During Mu'awiya ibn Abi Sufyan's governorship of Syria in the 640s, Samayfa was known to act counter to Mu'awiya's orders in Homs, where the Yemenite troops followed his command rather than the official governor's.

At the Battle of Siffin in 657 during the First Fitna, Samayfa commanded the Himyarite troops in Mu'awiya's army against Caliph Ali and was killed in combat on 27 July while leading his forces against the Rabi'a contingent. His death, along with that of Ubayd Allah ibn Umar, marked the beginning of Himyarite decline in Syria. Samayfa was succeeded by his son Shurahbil, who continued as a leading Umayyad commander until his death at the Battle of Khazir in 686, after which the Himyar in Syria 'sank to military insignificance' according to Madelung.

Other members of the Dhu al-Kala family served as governors and commanders for the Umayyad caliphs, including Khalid ibn Ma'dan ibn Abi Karib who served as head of Yazid I's shurta (select troops) and later became a prominent Muslim scholar, and Imran ibn al-Nu'man who served as governor of Sind under Caliph Umar II (717-720). The family maintained their significance as part of the broader Himyarite aristocratic presence in early Islamic Syria, contributing to Homs becoming a center of South Arabian settlement, culture, and political power.

data.history:
The Ghamid tribe traces its lineage to Ghamid (Amr ibn Ka'b ibn Al-Harith ibn Ka'b ibn Abdullah ibn Malik ibn Nasr), who is Shanu'ah of the Azd, belonging to the Qahtani lineage. They are considered one of the oldest and largest tribes of the Arabian Peninsula, with their historical homeland in the Al-Bahah region of southwestern Hejaz. The tribe is closely related to the neighboring Zahran tribe, both being part of Azd Shanu'ah. In pre-Islamic times, when Abraha the Abyssinian marched toward Mecca to destroy the Kaaba, Ghamid confronted and defeated his cavalry forces. The tribe sent two delegations to Prophet Muhammad - first in Mecca under Abi Dhibyan's leadership, and later in Madinah during the 10th year after Hijrah, where they embraced Islam and received written guarantees of their rights.

Throughout the 17th century, Ghamid participated extensively in military campaigns and tribal conflicts. In 1638 CE, they faced an invasion by Zaid bin Mohsen which ended in reconciliation. They engaged in battles against Banu Al-Harith, supported the Sharif of Mecca in various campaigns including the famous battle of Al-Dhafa'a in 1678, and fought alongside Sharif Muhammad Al-Harith against the Dhafir tribes in Al-Qassim. During the 18th-19th centuries, they actively resisted Ottoman-Egyptian forces, notably defeating a Turkish army of twenty thousand under Abdin Bey in the Quraish Valley in 1814. They participated in numerous campaigns alongside various rulers including Imam Faisal bin Saud in 1815, where they helped achieve victory against Turkish-Egyptian forces.

The tribe maintained their presence in their ancestral homeland while many members migrated during Islamic expansions, with portions settling in Iraq, Jordan, Oman, Sudan, Egypt, UAE, and Yemen. Today, many Ghamdis have adopted the surname Al-Ghamdi and have migrated to Saudi Arabia's major metropolitan centers of Riyadh, Jeddah, and Dammam, while maintaining their tribal identity and cultural heritage. The Ghamid people are predominantly Sunni and continue to be recognized as one of the significant tribes that has maintained both its name and tribal presence from pre-Islamic times to the present day.

data.history:
Hamdan is an ancient Qahtani tribal confederation first mentioned in Sabaean inscriptions dating to the 7th century BCE, where it was referred to as 'Ardam Hamdan' (the land of Hamdan), indicating their territorial control in the northern highlands of Yemen. The confederation stems from the eponymous ancestor Awsala (nicknamed Hamdan), whose lineage traces through the Qahtani line to Kahlan and ultimately to Saba. From the 4th century BCE, Hamdan kings began controlling the Kingdom of Sheba, with leadership alternating between their two main branches: Hashid (first mentioned as 'Hashdum' in 4th century BCE texts) and Bakil (referenced from 6th century BCE). Yarim Ayman of Hashid monopolized the Sabaean kingdom around the 2nd century BCE, before power shifted to Bakil in the 1st century BCE, ending when the Himyarites under Dhamar Ali Yahbar I triumphed in 100 CE.

During the Islamic period, Hamdan played crucial roles in early Muslim history. In 631 CE, Hamdan delegations led by poet Malik ibn Namat and prince Abu Thawr Dhu'l-Mashar met Prophet Muhammad seeking alliance, with more Hamdani tribesmen converting during Ali ibn Abi Talib's expedition to Yemen in 631-632. Muhammad appointed a Bakil tribesman as his deputy over Hamdan, and the tribe remained loyal during the Ridda wars following Muhammad's death in 632. Hamdan soldiers who settled in Kufa became fervent supporters of Ali during his caliphate (656-661), contributing 12,000 men to Ali's army at the Battle of Siffin in 657 under their leader Sa'id ibn Qays al-Hamdani.

This close association with Ali led Hamdan to embrace Shi'a Islam, initially following Isma'ilism before becoming supporters of Zaydism. The Banu Yam branch of Hamdan settled north of Bakil in Najran and established the Isma'ili Hamdanid dynasty at Sanaa in the 12th century, later shifting toward Zaydi acceptance. Geographically, their ancient territory extended from Sanaa toward Marib, Najran, Saada, and the Red Sea coast in the northern highlands of Yemen. Today, Hamdan comprises over one million members across Hashid and Bakil confederations, historically known as 'the wings of the Zaydite imamate' while maintaining wide-ranging autonomy. They continue to dominate northern Yemen from Sanaa to Saada, wielding significant political influence since the 1962 revolution despite the confederation's internal diversity and periodic shifts in tribal loyalties.

data.history: The Hourani clan derives its name from the Hauran region of southern Syria, with documented origins tracing to the Arab al-Halqiyyin tribe. According to historical records of the prominent Al-Hourani family, they moved from the town of Jasim in the southern Hawran region to Hama in central Syria around the 1510s, establishing their distinctive surname Al-Hourani based on their regional origin. The family claimed descent from Muhammad through genealogical records displayed in Hama's museum, though such genealogical claims require careful verification. The research indicates connections between Hourani Bedouins and the broader Mahamid tribal confederation, with the Hourani listed as a branch that emerged from the Mahamid's migrations from the Arabian Peninsula through the Levant. Syrian sources specifically identify Hourani Bedouins in the Daraa and Quneitra governorates, noting their close relationships with Jordanian tribes. The clan appears to represent descendants of migrants who integrated into existing tribal structures throughout the Arabian Peninsula and Gulf regions, maintaining clan identity while becoming part of larger tribal confederations. Their presence in Gulf communities reflects the broader pattern of Levantine migration to the region, though their numbers remain limited and scattered across different emirates. The Mahamid confederation itself traces origins to the Khawlaniyah al-Qahtaniyah Harb tribe, with the majority originally residing in Yemen, the Hijaz between Mecca and Medina, before spreading across the Arabian Peninsula and Maghreb regions.

The Banu Jusham (Arabic: بنو جشم) were a large Arabian sub-tribe descended from Jusham ibn Muawiya ibn Bakr ibn Hawazin, making them part of the larger Hawazin confederation within the Qays Aylan tribal group. As Adnanite Arabs tracing their lineage through Mudar, they were pastoral nomads who inhabited the steppes between Mecca and Medina during the pre-Islamic era. The tribe was frequently engaged in conflicts with rival tribes including the Banu Fazara and participated in the major inter-tribal warfare of their time.

During the late 6th century, the Banu Jusham joined other Hawazin tribes in the Fijar War against the Quraysh and Kinana confederation. This conflict arose after the murder of 'Urwa ibn al-Rahhal of the Banu 'Amir by al-Barrad ibn Qays al-Damri during a sacred season, leading to multiple battles over four years. The Hawazin, including Jusham, achieved several victories against their Qurayshi opponents at locations like Shamta and Ukaz before the war eventually concluded with a negotiated peace.

The Banu Jusham had minimal contact with Prophet Muhammad until 630 CE when they were defeated alongside other Hawazin tribes at the Battle of Hunayn. Following this defeat, they reconciled with the Muslim state under Muhammad's leadership. However, after Muhammad's death in 632, the Jusham joined other Hawazin tribes in the Ridda Wars, discontinuing tribute payments to Medina and fighting against the early Islamic state before ultimately returning to the Muslim fold.

The tribe later became part of the great Hilalian migration westward across North Africa in the 11th century. According to historical sources, the Banu Jusham formed one of the six major divisions of the Banu Hilal confederation during this migration period. After their defeat by the Almohads, they were settled alongside the Riyah tribe in regions of present-day western Morocco by the Almohad ruler Yaqub al-Mansur, with the Jusham specifically establishing themselves in the Tamasna region. Additionally, genealogical sources indicate that Jusham also formed one of the largest divisions within the Banu Taghlib's al-Araqim group, where they were known as one of 'al-Rawkan' (the two horns) alongside the Malik division due to their significant size and military strength. Through various tribal genealogies and migrations, the Jusham name became connected to multiple Arabian tribal branches, reflecting the complex intertribal relationships that characterized Arabian Peninsula tribal history.

data.history:
Khath'am was an ancient Arab tribal confederation that controlled territory in southwestern Arabia, particularly the mountainous regions between Najran and Ta'if, including Turubah, Bisha, Tabala, and Jurash, from at least the 6th century CE. The tribe's genealogical origins were disputed by medieval Arab historians - Ibn Hisham and Ibn Qutayba claimed they were Adnanites descended from Khath'am ibn Anmar ibn Nizar, while Ibn al-Kalbi held they were Qahtani descendants through Khath'am (birth name Aftal) ibn Anmar ibn Irash ibn Amr ibn al-Ghawth. Modern historian Giorgio Levi Della Vida suggests they were likely a confederation of smaller tribes with different origins. The main clans included Shahran, Nahish, and Aklub, the latter said to have descended from Rabi'a ibn Nizar and joined through alliance.

The Khath'am controlled strategic territory along the caravan route between Yemen and Mecca and worshipped at the cult of Dhu'l-Khalasa alongside the Bajila, Bahila, and Banu Daws tribes at Tabala. In the mid-6th century, they were involved in Abraha's expedition against Mecca, with conflicting accounts of their role. Their most famous pre-Islamic battle was the day of Fayf al-Rih, where under warrior poet Anas ibn Mudrik, they led an alliance including Madhhij that defeated the Banu Amir. The tribe also engaged in prolonged warfare with the Banu 'Amir confederation, with 'Amir ibn al-Tufayl's father al-Tufayl ibn Malik being slain in combat against the Khath'am, and 'Amir himself losing an eye at the battle of Fayf al-Rih.

Initially hostile to Islam, the Khath'am eventually sent a delegation accepting the religion, with Muhammad declaring all their pre-Islamic blood feuds abolished. A section rebelled during the Ridda Wars but ultimately submitted after the destruction of their Dhu'l-Khalasa shrine by Jarir ibn Abd Allah al-Bajali in 632 CE. According to the Book of Idols, when Jarir attacked the shrine, he was met by the Khath'am and Bajila who resisted and attempted to defend Dhu'l-Khalasa, resulting in the death of many Khath'am warriors, including two hundred from the Banu Qubafah ibn-'Amir ibn-Khath'am. Members of the tribe participated in the early Muslim conquests in Syria and Iraq during the 630s, forming part of the Arab garrisons of Kufa and Basra.

The tribe maintained connections with other southwestern Arabian tribes through intermarriage and alliances. Notable family connections included Umays ibn Ma'ad of the Khath'am, whose daughters included Asma bint Umays and Salma bint Umays, prominent figures in early Islamic history. In the Kufan military organization of 671 CE, Khath'am soldiers were assigned alongside other South Arabian tribes including Azd Sarat, Hadhramawt, Kinda, and Bajila, reflecting their continued South Arabian tribal associations. The tribe's descendants are found across the Arabian Peninsula today, including in GCC states.

data.history:
The Kunud (singular Al Kindi) is a tribe of the United Arab Emirates and Oman that traces its origins to the late Pre-Islamic Recent (PIR) period. According to tribal genealogy, the Kunud originated from the tribal confederation that fought alongside the Sasanian Empire's Lakhmid clients during this formative period of Arabian Peninsula history.

The tribe has been genealogically linked to Miqdad ibn Aswad Al Kindi, described as an immigrant who came to Oman from Yemen during the time of the Islamic Prophet Muhammad. This connection places the Kunud within the broader framework of Yemeni tribal migrations that occurred during the early Islamic period, though the specific details of this lineage require further verification from classical genealogical sources.

By the turn of the 20th century, the Kunud had established themselves as a settled population of approximately 1,500 individuals concentrated along the East Coast of the Arabian Peninsula. Their territorial distribution extended from Nizwa in Oman northward to Kalba in Sharjah and Al Hayl in Fujairah, as well as settlements in Mahdah and Buraimi. This geographic spread indicates the tribe's adaptation to both the interior oasis settlements and the coastal regions.

Religiously and culturally, the Kunud traditionally followed the Omani Ibadi faith, which distinguished them from many other tribal groups in the region. The tribe has maintained long-standing associations with the Sudan (Al Suwaidi), though the specific nature and historical development of this relationship requires additional documentation from primary sources to be fully understood.
