data.history: The Makatim are a subsection of the larger Bani Kaab Arab tribe, which has origins in the area around and north of the Omani Wilayat of Mahdah, and areas of the Emirates east of Buraimi, including Wadi Kadra, Wadi Hatta and Wadi Qor. Unlike some other Bani Kaab subsections such as the nomadic Drisah and Shwaihiyin, the Makatim had settled by the turn of the 20th century as part of a population of some 7,250 people, of whom 1,150 remained Bedouin. The Bani Kaab tribe, including the Makatim subsection, became involved in the complex political dynamics of southeastern Arabia. In 1844, they allied with Sheikh Khalifa bin Shakhbut Al Nahyan of the Bani Yas in a tribal confederation to drive Wahhabi forces from Buraimi. However, their allegiances shifted over time - by the 1940s, the Bani Kaab had aligned with the Saudis, with their Sheikh Obaid bin Jumah declaring to the Governor of Saudi Arabia's Eastern Province that 'Our territories are yours.' During the 1950s Buraimi Dispute, the Bani Kaab supported the Saudi incursion under Sheikh Rashid bin Hamad of the Al Bu Shamis and fought against British forces and the Trucial Oman Levies. The research also indicates there is a residential area called Khudra al-Makatim (Green of the Makatim) in the Wilayat of Al-Khaburah in Oman's North Al Batinah Governorate, with approximately 300 inhabitants as of 2010, suggesting continued Makatim presence in their traditional territories.

data.history: The Mazari represent multiple distinct tribal groups that share only the name 'Mazari' but have completely different origins and histories. The most prominent is the Baloch Mazari tribe, one of the oldest Baloch tribes whose name derives from the Balochi word 'mazar' meaning 'Tiger'. This tribe consists of two main branches: the Balachani of Baloch origin and the Kurd of Kurdish origin. The Mazari conquered an area known as Tuman Mazari, encompassing parts of present-day Sindh's Kashmore District, Punjab's Rojhan Tehsil, and surrounding areas, with their stronghold at Rojhan-Mazari in Pakistan's Rajanpur District. During British rule, the Mazari reached their golden era when their territory extended from Bhong to the Sulaiman Mountains and from Kashmore to Giamul, covering approximately 8,000 square kilometers. The tribe fought significant battles against the Sikh Empire, including the 1836 attack on Mithankot led by Sardar Karam Khan under Chief Mir Bahram Khan. Peace was achieved in 1838 when Mir Bahram Khan visited Lahore with 12,000 tribesmen and was received by Maharaja Ranjit Singh at Lahore Fort. Notable leaders included Sir Nawab Imam Buksh Khan Mazari, who received British honors for his loyalty and services on the western frontier. His descendants continued as tribal chiefs, with the most recent being Mir Balakh Sher Mazari (1928-2022), the 22nd Sardar and 7th Mir of the Mazaris, educated at Aitchison College and Oxford University. His brother Sherbaz Khan Mazari became a prominent Pakistani politician, serving as caretaker Prime Minister in 1993. Separately, the Emirati Mazari (Al Mazrouei) are an Arab Bedouin tribe that forms part of the Bani Yas confederation. According to the research, they are considered part of the Bani Yas tribal confederation originally from Bani Tamim lineage, settling primarily in Abu Dhabi and the Liwa Oasis where they owned date gardens and engaged in pearl diving and pastoralism. The 1968 Abu Dhabi census recorded 1,287 members of the Mazari subsection among the Bani Yas population of 5,884. The Emirati Mazari supported Zayed bin Khalifa against Wahhabi incursions in the 19th century and later played important roles in UAE politics and oil industry development.

data.history: The existing data indicates that al-Muleikab is documented as one of the Bedouin clans (Manasir al-Badiyah) of the Manasir tribe in Northern Sudan, specifically inhabiting the Bayuda Desert adjacent to the Fourth Cataract of the Nile. According to tribal genealogy, they belong to the nomadic division of the Manasir people and share grazing grounds of Abisba' and Sani with three other Bedouin Manasir clans: al-Khabra, al-Hamamir, and al-Kagbab. The Manasir trace their origins to multiple possible lineages according to oral tradition - some claim descent from Mansur bin Qahtan of the Southern Arabs (Qahtaniin), others from Mansur bin 'Aim of the Kawahla, and still others from al-Hankab clan descendants of King Sabir. However, the provided research materials do not contain any additional information about al-Muleikab specifically, focusing instead on broader Arab history, general tribal structures, and other Arab groups. Critical research suggests the Manasir, including al-Muleikab, represent a voluntary amalgamation over centuries between indigenous Nubian groups, Arab emigrants, and recruited tribal minorities. During the dry season, al-Muleikab and other Bedouin Manasir clans migrate to various grazing areas including the Kababish territory to the west, Wad Hamid grazing grounds in Ga'ali country, or Rubatab territory, following traditional nomadic patterns of herding goats, sheep and camels. The current research materials do not provide specific information about their Arabian Peninsula origins, migrations to Sudan, historical events, or detailed genealogical connections beyond what is already documented.

The Northern Bani Qitab emerged as a significant tribal force in the early 20th century conflicts across the Trucial States and Buraimi region. In 1877, they participated in coordinated tribal raiding alongside the Duru and Awamir, with the Northern Bani Qitab specifically targeting Abu Dhabi while their allies raided Dubai. Their strategic importance became evident through their control of key territories including Falayah, which served as a stronghold for their operations. During the 1920s, the Northern Bani Qitab became deeply involved in the complex tribal warfare that engulfed the Buraimi oasis region. They allied with the Duru and Awamir in conflicts against the Manasir and Bani Yas forces. In December 1923, when Sheikh Sultan bin Zayed Al Nahyan marched against the Duru, the Bani Qitab of Falayah joined a northern confederation that successfully ambushed a party of 20 riders from Abu Dhabi, killing seven and capturing the rest. This northern confederation, which included the Northern Bani Qitab, subsequently raised a force of 200 men and launched a successful raid on Buraimi while Sultan's forces retaliated by raiding Falayah. The tribe's involvement in these conflicts demonstrated their military capability and strategic positioning in the interior regions of what would become the northern UAE.

The Qurra represents a distinct branch of the Banu Hilal confederation, descended from Hilal ibn Amir of the Hawazin tribal group within the broader Adnani lineage. As part of the great Hilali tribal confederation, the Qurra were among the most notable sub-tribes alongside the Athbaj, Riyah, Jusham, Zughba, and Adi during the massive Arab migrations of the 10th-11th centuries CE. Historical sources estimate that approximately one million Arab nomads participated in these westward migrations to the Maghreb, fundamentally transforming the demographic and cultural landscape of North Africa.

The Qurra, as Bedouin pastoralists, played a significant role in the Arabization of the Maghreb following the Fatimid decision to send the Banu Hilal confederation westward in 1050 CE. Prior to this major migration, some Qurra factions had already established presence in North African territories - notably, a faction of Zughba settled in Tripoli with Banu Qurra during 1037-1038 CE, indicating early Qurra presence in the region before the main Hilali movement. In the 12th century, the Qurra were documented as occupying territories stretching across North Africa, with the Zughba tribe (their close allies) controlling areas from Tlemcen in the west to Algiers in the east.

The tribal structure of the Qurra followed the traditional Bedouin confederation model, maintaining their Adnani Arab lineage and pastoral lifestyle throughout their migrations. Unlike some other tribal groups that established centralized settlements, the Qurra integrated into the broader tribal confederations of North Africa rather than founding independent territorial states. Their migration pattern followed the classic westward movement of the Hilali tribes, moving from their original Arabian territories across Egypt and into the Maghreb, where they became part of the complex tribal politics of medieval North Africa.

The Qurra's historical trajectory demonstrates the broader pattern of Arab tribal expansion and settlement in North Africa during the medieval period. As part of the Banu Hilal confederation, they maintained their tribal identity and genealogical connections while adapting to new geographical and political environments. Their limited presence in the modern Gulf region reflects the permanent nature of their westward settlement, as most Qurra branches established themselves in the territories they conquered during the 11th century migrations, contributing to the lasting Arabization of the western Maghreb alongside other Hilali tribes.

data.history: The research provided does not contain information about the Rulers of Ras Al Khaimah or the Al Qasimi dynasty. The Wikipedia article on Dubai mentions that in the early 19th century, Dubai was located within Bani Yas tribal lands but was also near the control of the powerful Al Qasimi clan, causing both groups to assert authority over the town. The article notes that merchants moved from Lingeh to Dubai, including those who had settled in Ras Al Khaimah and Sharjah which had historical links with Lingeh through the Al Qawasim tribe. The Arabic Wikipedia article on Khor al Sham mentions that the Musandam Peninsula was historically considered under Al Qasimi influence, but the independent-minded Shihuh were difficult to rule and their northern villages were often separatist. The Shihuh were often in conflict with the Qasimi in both Sharjah and Ras Al Khaimah and were more willing to accept Muscat's sovereignty, leading to Musandam being generally recognized as Omani territory by the early 20th century. However, the research does not provide sufficient detail about the Al Qasimi rulers of Ras Al Khaimah specifically, their genealogy, internal structure, or comprehensive history to construct a detailed historical narrative.

The Sba'a (Al-Sba'a, "The Seven") is a sub-tribe of the great Anizah confederation, part of the Rabiʿa branch of the Adnanites. The Anizah confederation, including the Sba'a, has pre-Islamic origins and was among the tribes that supported Ali ibn Abi Talib at the Battle of Siffin, contributing 4,000 armored warriors. The Sba'a is organized into several sub-sections, including Al-Abedah led by Ibn Hudayb, Al-Rasaleen led by Ibn Aida, and the Mezrab section from which came Sheikh Medjuel el Mezrab.

In the mid-18th to early 19th centuries, the Sba'a entered the central Syrian plains as part of the broader Anizah migration northward from the Arabian Peninsula. They often conflicted with the Hasana tribe for territorial control over the Syrian plains. The Sba'a established themselves in the Syrian desert regions, with their traditional territories extending into areas around Salamiyah, where they formed loose alliances with Ismaili settlers in the mid-19th century to provide mutual aid against other Bedouin groups.

The tribe was led by various sheikhs, most notably Ibn Murshid as Sheikh of Sba'a, with sub-sections maintaining their own leadership structure. A significant political moment occurred in June 1930 when Sheikh Rakan bin Mershed of the Sba'a signed a peace treaty with Nuri bin Hazaa Al Shalaan, the powerful chief of the Ruwallah tribe, ending conflicts between these two Anizah sub-tribes that had been competing for influence in the Syrian desert.

The Sba'a's connection to broader regional politics was demonstrated through the marriage of Jane Digby, an English aristocrat, to Sheikh Medjuel el Mezrab of the Mezrab section of the Sba'a in the 1850s. This union, which lasted 28 years until Digby's death in 1881, highlighted the tribe's integration into the complex social fabric of 19th-century Syria. Digby adopted Arab dress, learned Arabic, and lived both in Damascus and in the nomadic desert lifestyle, spending half of each year in goat-hair tents with the tribe.
data.nameEtymology: Al-Sba'a means 'The Seven' in Arabic, suggesting the tribe was originally a confederation of seven allied tribes or tribal sections, reflecting the significance of the number seven in Arabian tribal tradition

data.history: The Southern Bani Qitab represent a branch of the larger Bani Qitab tribal confederation that became geographically and politically distinct from their northern kinsmen in the Arabian Peninsula. The tribe's historical significance emerges clearly from late 19th and early 20th century tribal politics, where they played important roles in regional conflicts and alliances. The Bani Qitab as a whole were a significant Bedouin confederation that maintained both northern and southern branches, with the southern section eventually settling in territories that became incorporated into Oman's administrative structure. During the late 19th century, the broader Bani Qitab confederation was actively involved in the complex web of tribal alliances and conflicts that characterized the region. In 1875, they were attacked by Zayed bin Khalifa Al Nahyan of Abu Dhabi at Dhank, when he sent a mounted force of 200 Manasir and Bani Hajir Bedouin against them. This attack was part of Zayed's broader campaign to increase Abu Dhabi's influence in the Buraimi area, and it led to the Bani Qitab applying to Dubai for help, resulting in a standoff between the opposing forces. The incident demonstrates the strategic importance of the Bani Qitab in the regional balance of power and their ability to secure external support when threatened. The tribal confederation maintained significant military capability and political influence well into the 20th century. During the early 1900s, conflicts arose between the Bani Qitab and the Na'im tribe, which required intervention from the Trucial Sheikhs. In September 1905, the first meeting between the five Trucial Sheikhs took place in Dubai specifically to settle the dispute between these two tribal groups. The Bani Qitab had sought support from Rashid bin Ahmad Al Mualla, the young ruler of Umm Al Quwain, who was eager to expand his influence among the interior tribes. A second meeting in April 1906 at Khawaneej resulted in a treaty that allocated tribal responsibilities, with Rashid bin Ahmad taking responsibility for the Bani Qitab, Ghafalah, and Bani Ka'ab tribes. The Southern Bani Qitab's eventual integration into Oman's administrative framework marked their formal separation from the broader tribal network that continued to operate across the Trucial States, distinguishing their historical trajectory from their northern relatives who maintained greater political autonomy in the emerging UAE.

Tasm was an ancient pre-Islamic Arabian tribe that dominated the region of Al-Yamama in the Arabian Peninsula alongside the Jadis tribe from approximately 110 BCE until their complete extinction in the 6th century CE. According to Arab genealogical tradition, Tasm descended from Tasm ibn Laud ibn Sam ibn Nuh, placing them among the earliest Qahtani tribes and classifying them as al-Arab al-Ba'ida (extinct Arabian tribes). Both Tasm and Jadis were contemporary to other ancient Arabian tribes including the Amalek, 'Ad, and Thamud, and established a significant civilization in Al-Yamama, constructing numerous palaces and fortresses.

By the 6th century CE, Tasm had gained dominance over their tribal neighbors, the Jadis, with both tribes ruled by a king named Imliq. This king implemented oppressive policies including droit du seigneur over Jadis virgins and control over trade routes, which created severe tensions between the two allied tribes. The oppression eventually led to a rebellion in which Jadis warriors assassinated King Imliq during a performance by revealing hidden swords from sand, marking the beginning of the end for both tribal entities.

Following the assassination of their king, both tribes faced retaliation from the Himyarite armies. According to Tabari and Ibn Sa'id al-Maghribi, the Jadis tribe failed to prepare for the incoming Himyarite forces despite warnings from a woman of their tribe, resulting in their complete massacre with only children taken as slaves. Tasm also became extinct around the same period in the 6th century CE, though the exact circumstances of their disappearance are unclear in historical sources.

After their extinction, the palaces and fortresses that Tasm and Jadis had constructed in Al-Yamama were taken over by the Banu Hanifa tribe. The legacy of Tasm and Jadis became part of the broader narrative of the al-Arab al-Ba'ida, representing the ancient pre-Islamic Arab peoples who disappeared before the rise of Islam through warfare, natural disasters, and political upheavals that characterized early Arabian Peninsula history. Their historical significance lies primarily in Arab tribal genealogical traditions and as examples of the tribal conflicts that shaped pre-Islamic Arabia.

The Utub (العتوب) are a tribal confederation that emerged from Najdi origins, with most clans including Al-Sabah and Al-Khalifa tracing their lineage to the Anizah tribe, while some branches like Al Bin Ali descend from Banu Sulaym. Both lineages trace back to Adnani roots through the patriarch Adnan, who is considered a descendant of Ishmael son of Abraham. During a prolonged drought in the 17th century, the Utub clans migrated from their homeland in the Najd region, particularly from the area of Al-Aflaj known as Al-Haddar, around 1674-1676. They traveled through Al Aflaj, Qatif, and Al Ahsa before reaching Freiha in northwestern Qatar where they established a fortified coastal hub with access to maritime trade routes and pearling waters.

After successive military conflicts with the dominant Al Musallam tribe under nominal Ottoman support, the Utub departed Qatar for Iraq near Basra in 1716. They were expelled from Umm Qasr by the Ottomans due to their raiding activities on caravans and ships in Shatt al-Arab. Following their expulsion, they migrated to Kuwait around 1715 and established a government under the Al-Sabah family with permission from Bani Khaled rulers. Around the 1760s, the Al Jalahma and Al Khalifa clans migrated to Zubarah in Qatar, leaving Al Sabah as sole proprietors of Kuwait. The Al Bin Ali had settled at Zubarah in 1732, founding and ruling the town and making it one of the most important pearl trading centers in the Persian Gulf.

The Utub achieved their greatest military success in 1783 when they launched an expedition from Zubarah and conquered Bahrain under the leadership of Ahmed bin Muhammad Al Khalifa and Salama bin Saif Al Bin Ali. About 2,000 Persian troops had attacked Zubarah on 17 May 1783, but after suffering defeat, they retreated. An Utub naval fleet from Kuwait arrived in Bahrain the same day and set Manama ablaze, ultimately overrunning the Persian garrisons. The Al Khalifa established ruling authority in Bahrain that continues today. The confederation also expanded their influence to East Africa, with the Al Bin Ali clan participating in the conquest of Mombasa in 1837 alongside the Sultan of Muscat and Oman.

Throughout their history, the Utub developed into skilled maritime traders, pearl merchants, and naval powers. They served various regional powers, assisting the Imam of Oman Sultan bin Saif II in invading Bahrain in 1717 to expel the Safavids, and even participated in the Safavid request to invade Oman in 1697. The confederation established themselves across multiple Gulf states: Al Sabah became the ruling family of Kuwait, Al Khalifa the ruling family of Bahrain, Al Bin Ali maintained significant presence in Qatar and parts of Saudi Arabia's Eastern Province, while Al Jalahma remained influential merchants and traders without establishing their own political entity.

folkLegends.title: Origin of the Name Utub
folkLegends.story: Some sources suggest the name derives from the verb 'ataba' meaning to migrate or move, applied because of their frequent migrations and movements from place to place. However, the name was already in use before their northern migration, as evidenced by Yusuf al-Bahrani referring to them as 'Utub' in 1700 when describing an attack on Bahrain that occurred before their move north.
folkLegends.source: Arabic historical sources and Yusuf al-Bahrani's Luluat al-Bahrain

folkLegends.title: Ancestral Origin from Utbah
folkLegends.story: The confederation is said to be named after their ancestor Utbah, with some sources tracing this to Utbah ibn Rabah ibn Hilal ibn Amir ibn Sa'sa'ah, while others trace Al Bin Ali specifically to Utbah ibn Khuffaf ibn Imru al-Qais ibn Bahthah ibn Sulaym.
folkLegends.source: Genealogical traditions recorded in Arabic sources

data.nameEtymology: The name 'Utub' (العتوب) has disputed origins. Some sources claim it derives from the ancestor Utbah, while others suggest it comes from the verb 'ataba' (عتب) meaning to migrate or move, due to their frequent relocations. However, evidence shows the name was used before their major northern migration, suggesting the migration theory may be incorrect.

The research reveals a fundamental discrepancy regarding the Zoubi tribal identity. The existing data describes the Zoubi as a Gulf Arabian tribe tracing lineage to Za'ab from northern Arabia, but the documented research shows the Zoubi (al-Zu'bi) as a definitively Levantine Arab clan centered in southern Syria, northern Jordan, and northern Israel. The documented Zoubi are an agricultural clan of approximately 160,000 members who claim descent from the family of Prophet Muhammad through their connection to Abdul Qadir Gilani (1077-1166), founder of the Qadiriyya Sufi order. Their ancestor Ahmad Ali Al Gilani was later titled Al-Zoubi, establishing the genealogical link between the Levantine Zoubi and the broader Gilani Sayyid lineage.

The Zoubi traditionally provided the leaders of the Qadiriyya Sufi order in the Hauran region of southern Syria, from which they derived their political prestige even after Sufism's decline in the early 20th century. They are the largest of the extended agricultural clans of the Hauran plains and inhabit sixteen villages in Syria's Daraa Governorate, including the capital Daraa and villages like Khirbet Ghazaleh, Tafas, al-Musayfirah, and Muzayrib. In Jordan, they have extensive presence in Irbid Governorate, particularly in al-Ramtha and Irbid. In Israel, the clan is predominant in villages east of Afula in the Jezreel and Beisan valleys, and forms one of the principal Muslim families in Nazareth.

During French Mandatory Syria (1920-1946), the Zoubi were rivals of the Hariri clan and aligned themselves with French authorities while the Hariri supported Syrian nationalism. This political alignment was partly due to the Sufi inclination toward governing authorities. Under Ba'ath Party rule post-1963, lower-ranking Zoubi members gained prominence, most notably Mahmoud Al-Zoubi who served as Syria's Prime Minister from 1987-2000. The clan's political influence subsequently declined under President Bashar al-Assad, who showed more favor to their traditional rivals, the Hariri.

In Palestine/Israel during the 1930s, the head of the Zoubi family was Muhammad Said, based in the village of Nein. During the 1936-1939 Palestinian revolt, the Zoubi largely opposed Amin al-Husayni. Muhammad Said and his relatives facilitated land sales to the Jewish National Fund, leading to assassination attempts against family members in 1947. During the 1948 Palestine war, the Zoubi forged peace agreements with neighboring Jewish settlements, with Seif el-Din el-Zoubi serving as an intermediary between Israeli authorities and Arab inhabitants of the Nazareth area.

data.history: The Banu Taghlib were a powerful pre-Islamic Arab tribe of Adnanite origin, descended from Taghlib ibn Wa'il through the genealogical line: Taghlib ibn Wāʾil ibn Qasit ibn Hinb ibn Afṣā ibn Duʿmī ibn Jadīla ibn Asad ibn Rabīʿa ibn Nizār ibn Maʿadd ibn Adnān. Originally Bedouin inhabitants of the Najd, they belonged to the Rabi'a confederation and were among the most powerful and cohesive nomadic tribes of pre-Islamic Arabia. The tribe was organized around the al-Araqim, descendants of six sons of Bakr ibn Hubayb ibn Amr ibn Ghanm, with the largest divisions being Jusham and Malik, collectively known as al-Rawkān ('the two horns'). In the late 5th century, the assassination of their chieftain Kulaib ibn Rabiah by Jassas ibn Murra al-Shaybani of Banu Bakr precipitated the famous Basus War, a forty-year conflict that ended with the Taghlib's decisive defeat at Yawm al-Tahaluq, forcing the bulk of the tribe to flee Najd for the Lower Euphrates region. The tribe migrated further north to Upper Mesopotamia (Jazira) after their chieftain Amr ibn Kulthum assassinated the Lakhmid king Amr ibn al-Hind in 568 CE. Unlike most Arab tribes, the Banu Taghlib embraced Miaphysite Christianity and remained largely Christian long after the advent of Islam. During the early Muslim period, they initially opposed the Muslims during the Ridda Wars (632-633) but later switched allegiance while retaining their Christian faith. Notably, they refused to pay jizya like other Christian subjects and successfully negotiated with Caliph Umar to pay zakat or sadaqah instead, arguing they were Arabs and should be treated like Muslims. During the Umayyad period (661-750), they allied with the Umayyads against the Qaysi tribes, with their poet al-Akhtal serving as the official court poet. Mass conversion to Islam occurred in the second half of the 9th century during Abbasid rule, partly through the persuasion of Malik ibn Tawk. The tribe reached its political zenith through the Hamdanid dynasty, descendants of the Adi ibn Usama line, who established autonomous emirates in Mosul (930s) and Aleppo (945) until their demise in 1002. After the early Hamdanid period, the Taghlib disappeared from historical records as a distinct tribal entity, with modern sources suggesting their descendants assimilated into the Anizah tribe, specifically the Banu Bishr branch in the Najd region.

folkLegends.title: The Basus War Origin
folkLegends.story: The forty-year Basus War began when Kulaib ibn Rabiah of Taghlib killed a camel belonging to a woman named Basus, who was related to Jassas ibn Murrah of Banu Bakr. The lament of Basus, presented poetically, provoked Jassas to kill Kulaib in revenge, setting off retaliatory violence between the two tribes that lasted for decades.

data.nameEtymology: Named after the progenitor Taghlib ibn Wa'il, also known as Dithar ibn Wa'il

migrationPath.description: Mass exodus after decisive defeat at Yawm al-Tahaluq during Basus War

timelineEvents.title: Assassination of Kulaib ibn Rabiah
timelineEvents.description: Kulaib ibn Rabiah, Taghlibi chieftain, murdered by Jassas ibn Murra al-Shaybani of Banu Bakr, precipitating the Basus War

timelineEvents.title: Battle of Yawm al-Tahaluq
timelineEvents.description: Decisive defeat of Banu Taghlib by Banu Bakr, forcing bulk of tribe to flee Najd

timelineEvents.title: Assassination of Amr ibn al-Hind
timelineEvents.description: Taghlibi chieftain Amr ibn Kulthum assassinated Lakhmid king, leading to northward migration

timelineEvents.title: Battle of Dhi Qar
timelineEvents.description: Taghlib fought alongside Sasanians against Banu Bakr

timelineEvents.title: Ridda Wars Opposition
timelineEvents.description: Taghlib initially fought alongside apostate tribes against Muslim armies

timelineEvents.title: Battle of Yawm al-Hashshak
timelineEvents.description: Decisive Taghlibi victory against Qaysi tribes, killing Sulaymi chief Umayr ibn al-Hubab

timelineEvents.title: Hamdanid Emirate of Mosul
timelineEvents.description: Nasir al-Dawla of Hamdanids (Taghlibi descendants) formed autonomous emirate

timelineEvents.title: Hamdanid Emirate of Aleppo
timelineEvents.description: Sayf al-Dawla established northern Syrian emirate based in Aleppo

timelineEvents.title: End of Hamdanid Rule
